cover.title: THE BLADE
cover.subtitle: The one who sees the pattern early, then gives it one more chance.
cover.tagline: You already know what matters — but hope keeps asking for one more confirmation.

profile_scores.caption: High drive and pride, low warmth — you trust slowly and judge accurately, but standards come before warmth.

short_summary: You trust slowly because you know actions matter more than nice words.
Your pattern is not missing the sign — it's seeing it early and still giving the relationship one more chance.
What changes love for you is trusting your first judgment before the explanation becomes more convincing than the behavior.

full_report.headline: THE BLADE
full_report.intro: You're not someone who trusts people quickly. You watch how they act, what they repeat, and whether they keep their promises. You often see the problem early — but you stay to confirm it one more time. This report follows that pattern: why you keep understanding what should have been enough, who pulls you into second chances, and how to trust your own judgment sooner.

chapters.title: When You Love
body: You're not someone who trusts people quickly.
You don't give everything from the start.
You don't say everything from the start, either.
Instead, you watch.
How they act. What they keep repeating. Whether they keep their promises. Or don't.
People sometimes think you're cold.
But that's wrong.
You're not cold — you're checking.
Because you know. Actions matter more than nice words.
So you're careful about who you choose.
But once you choose, you stay. You don't give up easily. You don't leave easily.
That's why it hurts more. You don't love just anyone.
chapters.profile_note: High Drive · High Pride, Low Warmth — You trust slowly and judge accurately, but standards come before warmth.

chapters.title: The Pattern You Repeat
body: This might feel strange.
You're pretty good at reading relationships.
You know where the problem is. You know what the other person is avoiding. You roughly know where this relationship is headed.
But even knowing that, you stay.
It feels like things might change if you watch a little longer. It feels like things might get better if you wait a little longer. It feels like you might understand if you talk a little more.
So time passes.
And in the end you confirm that the problem you saw at the start was right all along.
That moment is the emptiest.
Not because you didn't know. But because you knew from the beginning.
chapters.highlight: The reason you repeat this isn't that you're oblivious. It's that your wish to double-check is too strong.

chart.title: THE SECOND CHANCE CURVE
chart.description: The pattern of giving the relationship one more chance even after spotting the problem.

chapters.title: What You Got Wrong About Yourself

items.belief: I have no eye for people.
items.truth: No. It's the opposite. You see things too early. The problem isn't that you can't see — it's that you ignore what you saw.
items.truth_short: You don't miss the sign. You often see it early, then ignore it.

items.belief: This time I really didn't know.
items.truth: Think about it. The moment you first felt something was off. It was there, wasn't it? You already knew then. You just didn't want to believe it.
items.truth_short: The first uneasy moment was already information. You just didn't want to believe it yet.

items.belief: Why do I keep making the same mistake?
items.truth: You're not repeating a mistake. You're repeating the checking. The hope that this time might be the exception. That's what pulls you back in.
items.truth_short: You're not repeating the mistake. You're repeating the need to check one more time.

chapters.title: The Person You Keep Choosing
body: You're not drawn to someone perfect from the start.
You're drawn to someone who needs understanding.
Someone with a reason. A cause. An explanation that makes sense.
Because you're someone who sees context.
But here's the problem.
A behavior that can be explained and a behavior that's okay are two different things.
chapters.highlight: Someone who tries to convince you gives a reason. Someone who loves you changes their behavior.

chapters.title: What Actually Changes Something
body: What you need isn't more analysis.
You already do plenty of that.
What you need is trust.
Trust in your own judgment.
The moment something first felt off. The moment something first felt wrong. That first snag.
You can trust that more.
It's been right more often than you think.
chapters.action_sentence: You can trust that more. It's been right more often than you think.

chapters.title: Where to Go From Here
body: You know how to understand a relationship.
You know how to read a situation.
You know how to read a person.
But what you'll learn next is how to tell the difference between an explanation and a change.
Anyone can give a reason.
But not everyone actually changes.
Once you start seeing that difference, relationships become much simpler.
chapters.transition_note: You've spent a long time learning how to understand people. The next report covers why you keep passing over signs you already recognize, and how you can start trusting that judgment.

chapters.title: The Type You Keep Falling For

items.type_title: Someone with a lot of backstory
items.description: You hear about everything they've survived before you really know who they are now. Their pain makes sense. Their behavior makes sense too. But making sense of it doesn't automatically make it okay.
The conversations last for hours. Every decision has a reason. Every problem has an explanation. But when the same issue returns again, you realize a relationship isn't a presentation.
You see who they could become more clearly than who they are today. The future feels bright enough to justify the present. But the future hasn't arrived yet.
They look sincere. They know exactly what went wrong. It's a good start. But admitting and changing are still two different things.

chapters.title: The Warning Signs You've Missed
chapters.intro_note: Most of these showed up early. You just kept finding reasons for them.

signal_strength.label: There's a small gap between what they say and what they do
There are moments they treat promises lightly
There's always an explanation, but never any change

chapters.core_signal: You — the one who already understands the problem — keep explaining it to yourself, over and over.

chapters.title: What a Healthy Relationship Looks Like to You

checklist.title: Mistakes don't repeat.
checklist.description: Once can be a mistake. Twice is a pattern.

checklist.title: They don't have to prove trust.
checklist.description: They're already showing it through action.

checklist.title: Your mind feels at ease.
checklist.description: This matters more than you think. If you're still analyzing constantly, something's probably already off.

chapters.title: What Happens in Your Next Relationship

sections.who_breaks_you: You're weak to people who explain a lot. There's a reason. There's logic. There's a backstory. So it makes sense to you. But in the end, what actually matters isn't that. It's whether their behavior changes. Or doesn't. That's it.
sections.who_steadies_you: On the other hand, there's someone who fits you well. Someone who fixes their mistakes. Someone who keeps their promises. Someone who shows they're sorry through action, not words. This kind of person doesn't wear out your mind.
sections.next_signs: This time, they'll show up faster. What used to take months before. This time it'll show up within weeks. The gap between words and actions. How they handle responsibility. And your own gut feeling. From the very start.
sections.try_this: This time, don't listen to the explanation. Just once. Just this one time. Wait before hearing the reason. And watch the behavior instead. Whether they actually change. Or just explain again. The Blade's problem isn't that you can't see people. You already saw it — you just gave them one more chance to be believed. This time, before you trust the other person, trust your own judgment first.

action_plan.summary: You already know how to read people. What's left is learning to trust what you saw before the explanation talks you out of it.
action_items: When something feels off, write down the behavior before listening to the reason.
Treat repeated explanations without changed behavior as information, not as progress.
Before giving one more chance, ask whether this is new evidence or the same pattern again.

closing.message: You've spent a long time learning how to understand people. The next report covers why you keep passing over signs you already recognize, and how you can start trusting that judgment.
closing.cross_sell_cta: Curious what your partner's pattern looks like? See their Cosmic Blueprint.
